clean_story: Glass knives from volcanoes.  Sharper than steel, born in fire. "Obsidian blades" are razor edges chipped from volcanic glass—a rock so pure it fractures into slices 500x thinner than a human hair. Imagine snapping a frozen lake’s surface—except the ice is black, harder than copper, and cuts like a laser. When lava cools instantly, atoms lock in chaos, no time to form crystals. The result?A material with no grain—just a uniform, brittle sharpness. Ancient surgeons in Mesoamerica used these blades for operations. Tests show obsidian scalpel wounds heal with less scarring than steel cuts—its edge is so fine it severs cells cleanly, like a guillotine through silk. Today, some eye surgeons still prefer obsidian for delicate incisions. No metal alloy matches its precision at microscopic scales. Three related subjects are volcanic tools, pre-Columbian surgery and ultra-sharp materials.
